facts.label: AFRICA'S FIRST GOALS
facts.story: On 27 May 1934, in Egypt's first-ever World Cup match, forward Abdulrahman Fawzi scored twice in a 2–4 defeat to Hungary, becoming the first African player to find the net at a World Cup. His feat stood entirely alone for eighty-four years — no other African forward managed two goals in a single World Cup match until Mohamed Salah matched him against Saudi Arabia in 2018, closing a gap that had outlasted entire generations of the sport.

facts.label: OLDEST MAN STANDING
facts.story: On 25 June 2018, goalkeeper Essam El-Hadary walked out against Saudi Arabia at 45 years and five months old, becoming the oldest player ever to appear in a World Cup match. Recalled for Egypt's dead-rubber finale after being benched for the first two games, he saved Salman Al-Faraj's first-half penalty to a raucous ovation. Egypt still lost 1–2, but El-Hadary's record — and that save — outlived the scoreline entirely.

1934.headline: Pharaohs' Historic Debut

annotations.label: FAWZI'S HISTORIC DOUBLE
annotations.story: In Egypt's first-ever World Cup match, Abdulrahman Fawzi scored twice in a 2–4 loss to Hungary, becoming Africa's first World Cup goalscorer. The tally stood alone for 84 years — no other African player scored twice in a match until Mohamed Salah in 2018.

annotations.label: FOUR DAYS BY SEA
annotations.story: With only one World Cup slot reserved for all of Africa and Asia combined, Egypt first had to beat Palestine in a two-legged playoff, winning 11–2 on aggregate. Goalkeeper Mustafa Kamel Mansour later recalled a four-day voyage aboard the steamship Helwan just to reach Italy for the tournament itself.

annotations.label: THE SHIRT THAT VANISHED
annotations.story: No verified specimen of Egypt's 1934 jersey — a long-sleeved green shirt with a simple red chest patch — is known to survive; historians work only from grainy photographs and hand-drawn kit charts. When replicas surface at auction, collectors treat them as artifacts of African football's very first World Cup jersey.

1934.homeColors: green long-sleeved shirt with a red chest patch and white shorts

1990.headline: The 56-Year Wait

annotations.label: ABDELGHANI STUNS THE DUTCH
annotations.story: Trailing Wim Kieft's 58th-minute header, Egypt won a penalty when Ronald Koeman fouled inside the box. Magdi Abdelghani stepped up in the 83rd minute and beat the Dutch keeper, salvaging a 1–1 draw against the reigning European champions in Egypt's first World Cup match in 56 years.

annotations.label: THE BACKPASS BORE
annotations.story: A goalless stalemate against Ireland in Palermo was so tedious — both goalkeepers repeatedly picking up backpasses to kill time — that FIFA's lawmakers intervened. Within two years, the backpass rule banning goalkeepers from handling deliberate passes from teammates entered the game, arguably football's single most consequential rule change.

annotations.label: A LOOK REVIVED
annotations.story: The 1990 kit's red home shirt and green away shirt became so tied to Egypt's return from a 56-year absence that Adidas revisited its design language for the 2018 comeback kit — a checkered red home shirt and striped white away shirt built as a deliberate nod to the 1990 look.

1990.homeColors: red shirt with a white V-neck collar and sleeve panels, white three stripes on the shoulders (Adidas)

2018.headline: Salah's Lonely Heroics

annotations.label: SALAH BREAKS 28-YEAR DROUGHT
annotations.story: Egypt trailed hosts Russia 3–0 in Saint Petersburg when Mohamed Salah converted a 73rd-minute penalty — the country's first World Cup goal since Abdelghani's strike in 1990. It arrived too late to matter in the scoreline, but not to a stadium full of Egyptian fans.

annotations.label: SALAH'S FAREWELL CHIP
annotations.story: Already eliminated, Mohamed Salah lifted a delicate 22nd-minute chip over goalkeeper Yasser Al-Mosailem, becoming the first African player to score in his first two World Cup appearances — scant consolation in a 1–2 defeat to Saudi Arabia that closed out Egypt's tournament.

annotations.label: THE RAMOS COLLISION
annotations.story: Weeks before the opener, Sergio Ramos wrenched Mohamed Salah's shoulder to the turf in the Champions League final, forcing him off after half an hour with ligament damage. Salah missed Egypt's opening loss to Uruguay entirely and played the remaining two matches still short of full fitness.

2018.homeColors: red shirt with black ribbed collar and sleeve cuffs, white stripes down the sides, subtle chequered pattern (Adidas)
2018.awayColors: white shirt with subtle graphic print, black shorts, white socks (Adidas)
